brief_justification: Arrives at the correct answer (Yes) with valid and specific statistical reasoning about relative vs absolute measures; goes three levels deep — identifies the core statistical ambiguity, enumerates concrete scenarios, then audits other metrics for analogous weaknesses; some awareness shown by explicitly partitioning the report into stronger vs weaker claims, though no explicit naming of cognitive biases or mid-analysis course-correction; verifies from multiple angles by enumerating three gap-narrowing scenarios and separately stress-testing graduation rates, spending, and special ed claims; clearly distinguishes what the data proves vs implies, never overstating confidence, and notes where ELL data is genuinely stronger; systematically evaluates all three mathematical scenarios for gap narrowing and considers alternative explanations for graduation rates, spending efficiency, and satisfaction surveys; every conclusion traces to an explicit derivation with enough specificity that a third party could fully reproduce the reasoning chain.
